facts.text: Mamaroneck was officially incorporated as a town on March 7, 1788, making it one of Westchester County’s oldest municipalities.
The first bloodshed of the American Revolution in Westchester County occurred here when Loyalist William Lounsbery was killed on August 29, 1776.
During the early 1920s, Mamaroneck became a hub of silent‑film production, with D. W. Griffith’s studio drawing stars like Mary Pickford and Charlie Chaplin.
Walter’s Hot Dog Stand, a local landmark since the 1930s, earned a spot on the National Register of Historic Places in 2010.
The area now known as Mamaroneck was first purchased from the Siwanoy Indians in 1661 by Englishman John Richbell.
In the late 19th and early 20th centuries, waves of German, Irish, Italian, and other European immigrants settled in Mamaroneck, shaping its cultural fabric.
